name: Smørrebrød
description: Open-faced rye bread sandwiches — Denmark's most iconic food. Topped with pickled herring, cold cuts, eggs, liver pâté or shrimp. An art form with strict topping conventions

name: Flæskesteg
description: Crispy roast pork with crackling — the quintessential Danish Christmas dinner main course. The crackling (svær) must be perfectly crisp. A point of national pride

name: Æbleskiver
description: Spherical pancake-like confections cooked in a special pan. Eaten during Christmas season dusted with powdered sugar and dipped in strawberry jam. Origin of word means 'apple slices'

name: Rugbrød
description: Dense sourdough rye bread — the backbone of Danish cuisine. Darker, denser and more nutritious than most European breads. Essential for smørrebrød

name: Wienerbrød (Danish Pastry)
description: Flaky laminated pastry dough pastries that the world calls 'Danish'. In Denmark itself there are dozens of varieties — spandauer, kanelsnegle (cinnamon rolls), kringle and more

name: Stegt Flæsk med Persillesovs
description: Fried slices of pork belly served with boiled potatoes and parsley sauce — voted Denmark's national dish in 2014 in a public poll

name: Frikadeller
description: Pan-fried meatballs made from ground pork and veal, onion and egg. A beloved everyday comfort food served with potatoes and red cabbage

name: Risalamande
description: Christmas rice pudding mixed with whipped cream, vanilla and chopped almonds. The tradition: whoever finds the whole hidden almond wins a prize

name: New Nordic Cuisine
description: Culinary movement pioneered by Noma (named world's best restaurant multiple times). Celebrates local, seasonal Scandinavian ingredients — foraged herbs, fermented vegetables, Nordic seafood

name: Danish Hotdog (Ristet Pølse)
description: Red sausage in a bun sold from iconic orange sausage wagons (pølsevogne) across Danish cities. The purist version: squiggly mustard, ketchup, raw onion, crispy onions, remoulade and cucumber
